title: Visual tokenization
description: How images and video frames become patches, discrete codes, or latent tokens so sequence-based models can read visual data.
openingSummary: Visual tokenization turns raw pixels from an image or video frame into a sequence of units a transformer, autoregressive, diffusion, or video model can process, and the main design choice is whether those units are spatial patches, discrete codebook symbols, or compressed latent features.

whatItIs.title: What It Is
whatItIs.body: Visual tokenization is the step that converts image or video-frame pixels into a sequence of model-readable units. Text models start from tokenizer output; visual models need an equivalent bridge because attention and autoregressive heads expect sequences, not raw raster grids. The output can be patch embeddings, discrete visual codes, or latent feature tokens, but the shared job is the same: turn a two- or three-dimensional visual field into an ordered list the rest of the stack can mix, predict, or denoise.

whyItMatters.title: Why It Matters
whyItMatters.body: The representation choice shapes compute cost, memory use, and what kinds of generation are practical. Patch tokens keep local spatial structure visible to attention but grow with image resolution. Discrete codes compress visuals into symbol sequences that autoregressive models can predict token by token. Latent tokens trade spatial detail for a smaller working space where diffusion or transformer updates are cheaper than editing every pixel. Video adds time, so frame, tubelet, or latent temporal tokens decide how motion is packed into the same sequence machinery.

representationForms.title: Main Representation Forms
representationForms.body:
Patch tokenization splits an image or frame into a grid of spatial regions and projects each region into an embedding that becomes one position in a sequence. Discrete-code tokenization maps visual content into learned codebook IDs so autoregressive systems can predict the next visual symbol the same way they predict text tokens. Latent-token approaches represent compressed image or video features that diffusion and transformer systems can update more cheaply than raw pixels, often after an encoder shrinks the visual field into a lower-resolution latent grid.

The comparison table below contrasts the three families by what visual data they start from, what each token looks like, where models typically use them, and the main tradeoff each choice introduces.

simpleExample.title: Simple Example
simpleExample.body: Take one photograph of a street scene. A patch tokenizer might cut the frame into a 16×16 grid of small squares, turn each square into one embedding vector, and feed a 256-position sequence to a vision transformer. A discrete-code model might instead encode the same scene into a shorter string of codebook IDs and ask an autoregressive head to predict the next ID. A latent diffusion setup might compress the image into a coarse latent map and run many small denoising updates there instead of on full-resolution pixels.

commonConfusions.title: Common Confusions
commonConfusions.body: Visual tokenization is not the same as text tokenization: both produce sequences, but visual methods must preserve spatial or compressed visual structure rather than word boundaries. Patch tokens are not the same as discrete visual codes; patches are continuous embeddings per region, while discrete codes are symbolic IDs from a learned vocabulary. Latent tokens are also not automatically smaller patch tokens; they usually come from a trained encoder that decides what detail to keep. Finally, one image can pass through more than one representation over a full system, such as patch encoding for understanding and latent tokens for generation.

representationFormsComparison.caption: How patch tokens, discrete visual codes, and latent visual tokens differ by source data, token form, common model use, and tradeoff.

patchTokens.sourceData: Image or video-frame pixels divided into a spatial grid of regions
patchTokens.tokenForm: Continuous embedding vector per patch region
patchTokens.commonModelUse: Vision transformers, CLIP-style image encoders, and multimodal understanding stacks
patchTokens.mainTradeoff: Keeps local spatial structure visible to attention, but sequence length grows with image resolution

discreteCodes.sourceData: Image or frame content after encoder quantization into a learned codebook
discreteCodes.tokenForm: Integer codebook ID per visual unit, read as a symbol sequence
discreteCodes.commonModelUse: Autoregressive visual generation that predicts the next visual code like text tokens
discreteCodes.mainTradeoff: Compresses visuals into a compact symbol stream, but reconstruction detail depends on codebook size and training

latentTokens.sourceData: Encoder-compressed image or video features rather than raw pixels
latentTokens.tokenForm: Lower-resolution latent feature map positions updated as tokens
latentTokens.commonModelUse: Latent diffusion and denoising transformers that iterate on visual latents instead of full-resolution pixels
latentTokens.mainTradeoff: Makes iterative generation cheaper than pixel-space edits, but fine detail depends on the encoder and decoder pair
